1.10: Onesimus was enslaved in Philemon's household in Colossae (cf. Col 4:9: 'Onesimus, our faithful and dear brother, who is one of you'). The traditional reading: Onesimus ran away from Philemon, somehow encountered Paul in prison (Rome or Ephesus), was converted, and is now being returned. A minority reading (B. Winter, A. Callahan): Onesimus was sent by Philemon to assist Paul in prison (a known practice, cf. Phil 2:25-30 — Epaphroditus sent to serve Paul), and Paul now appeals for him to be released permanently for gospel ministry. Either way, the letter navigates Roman slave-law (the Lex Petronia of ca. 61 CE and related legislation governed runaways) while applying theological pressure through the rhetoric of Christian brotherhood.
1.16: Roman slavery in the first century CE: approximately 1-2 million enslaved people in Italy alone (roughly 30-35% of the Italian population, Scheidel's estimate); enslaved persons could be freed by manumission (manumissio) through various legal mechanisms. Freed persons became Roman citizens with some legal restrictions. The distinction Paul makes — 'both in the flesh and in the Lord' — is the Christian social ontology: Onesimus has both a new spiritual identity (brother in Christ) and an unchanged social location (in Philemon's household). Paul does not directly call for legal manumission, but the category 'dear brother' both in the flesh and in the Lord strains against the institution's dehumanizing premise.
1.1: Paul writes from prison — 'a prisoner of Christ Jesus' — with Timothy as co-sender. The imprisonment is traditionally located in Rome (c. 60–62 CE), though Ephesus and Caesarea have been proposed. Philemon is addressed as 'dear friend (agapētos) and fellow worker (synergos),' placing him within the Pauline mission network as a co-laborer, not merely a convert. This opening triadic framing — prisoner, dear friend, fellow worker — establishes the letter's entire rhetorical appeal: Paul will leverage each element in turn to move Philemon without commanding him.
1.2: Apphia is addressed as 'our sister' (adelphē) — most likely Philemon's wife, since ancient letter convention grouped family members and since Col 4:17 places Archippus in the same household sphere. Archippus is 'our fellow soldier' (systratiōtēs) — the same military metaphor Paul uses of Epaphroditus in Phil 2:25; Archippus appears in Col 4:17 as someone charged to 'complete the ministry (diakonia) you have received in the Lord.' The house church (kat' oikon sou ekklēsia) meeting in Philemon's home was the standard early Christian assembly unit: no dedicated buildings existed; Christian community was embedded in household structures.
1.3: The standard Pauline grace-peace greeting derives from the Jewish shalom greeting fused with the Greek charis (which echoes the Roman salve/vale and the official rescript formula). 'God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ' as joint source of grace and peace is the Pauline theological grammar that brackets the entire letter: what Paul asks Philemon to do stands within the prior gift of divine grace and peace, not merely within human friendship.
1.4: 'I always thank my God as I remember you in my prayers' — the thanksgiving section (eucharistō) is a standard feature of Pauline letters and Hellenistic epistolary convention. The personal framing ('my God') reflects the intimacy of individual petitionary prayer in Second Temple Judaism and early Christianity. Remembering fellow believers in prayer was Paul's consistent practice (Rom 1:9-10; Phil 1:3-4; 1 Thess 1:2) and functioned as the epistolary equivalent of physical presence within networks separated by distance.
1.5: 'Because I hear about your love for all his holy people and your faith in the Lord Jesus' — the word order is chiastic: love-faith / holy people-Lord Jesus. The 'holy people' (hagioi) — the standard Pauline term for Christians — are the beneficiaries of Philemon's love; his faith is directed toward Jesus. Philemon's reputation for generosity to the wider community is attested by this verse: Paul has 'heard about' it, suggesting Philemon's hospitality was known across the Pauline network, not merely local.
1.6: 'I pray that your partnership (koinōnia) with us in the faith may be effective (energēs) in deepening your understanding of every good thing we have in Christ.' The koinōnia term here is the same word used for financial partnership in Phil 4:15 and for the Jerusalem collection in Rom 15:26 — it encompasses both spiritual fellowship and material sharing. The prayer that this partnership become 'effective' sets up the letter's request: for Philemon's partnership to become effective specifically in the case of Onesimus.
1.7: 'Your love has given me great joy and encouragement, because you, brother, have refreshed the hearts (splanchna) of the Lord's holy people.' The term splanchna (literally bowels/entrails) was the ancient seat of deep emotion — sympathy, compassion. Philemon has refreshed the splanchna of many believers; Paul will use this same word in v. 12 ('who is my very heart/splanchna') of Onesimus, and again in v. 20 ('refresh my heart/splanchna') in his appeal. The term is the emotional thread that ties the entire appeal together.
1.8: 'Although in Christ I could be bold (parrēsian) and order you to do what you ought to do' — parrēsia was the Athenian civic virtue of free speech, the right to speak plainly in the assembly. Paul's apostolic authority gives him parrēsia — the right to issue binding directives. The rhetorical move of naming the authority he is not using is a sophisticated Greco-Roman captatio benevolentiae (winning good will from the audience) that simultaneously reminds Philemon of Paul's standing.
1.9: 'Yet I prefer to appeal to you on the basis of love. It is as none other than Paul — an old man (presbytēs) and now also a prisoner of Christ Jesus.' The self-identification as presbytēs (old man) has been debated — some manuscripts read presbeutes (ambassador), but old man fits the pathos appeal: Paul leverages his age, his imprisonment, and the relationship of love to achieve what bare command could not. Greco-Roman rhetoric recognized that an appeal based on relationship and shared affection was more effective than command where social equals were involved; Philemon is a free householder, not Paul's subordinate.
1.11: 'Formerly he was useless (achrestos) to you, but now he has become useful (euchrestos) both to you and to me.' The wordplay is deliberate and culturally rich: Onesimus means 'useful' in Greek (onēsimos from oninēmi, to benefit). The name itself is a slave-name given to mark the owner's expectation of service. Paul's pun — useless/useful — plays against the name-ideology of Roman slavery. The additional resonance: achrestos sounds like a-Christos (without Christ) and euchrestos sounds like eu-Christos (good Christ/useful in Christ) — a phonetic reinforcement of the conversion narrative.
1.12: 'I am sending him — who is my very heart (ta ema splanchna) — back to you.' The legal formality of 'I am sending him back' (anapempō) was the technical verb for returning a runaway slave or a disputed person to their legal owner or the proper jurisdiction. Paul uses the formal legal language while simultaneously investing it with the deepest emotional content: the one he sends back is his own entrails, his deepest affection. The collision of legal formality and parental anguish is the rhetorical center of the letter.
1.13: 'I would have liked to keep him with me so that he could take your place in helping me while I am in chains for the gospel.' The phrase 'take your place' (hyper sou) implies that Philemon himself, had he been with Paul, would have served as a ministry partner; Onesimus, as Philemon's representative, could perform that service vicariously. The use of enslaved persons as proxies for free persons in legal and social contexts was common in Roman practice. Paul's statement implicitly frames Onesimus's service as Philemon's own service — a rhetorical move that obligates Philemon to consider the request as if it were his own action.
1.14: 'But I did not want to do anything without your consent, so that any favor you do would not seem forced but would be voluntary (kata hekousion).' The distinction between compelled (kata anagkēn) and voluntary (kata hekousion) action was fundamental to Greco-Roman ethics, law, and friendship. A gift extracted by obligation carried no moral value; only a freely-given benefit created the reciprocal goodwill that friendship required. Paul is carefully constructing the conditions under which Philemon's act of manumission or acceptance will be genuinely meritorious — not a legal obligation but a free exercise of Christian love.
1.15: 'Perhaps the reason he was separated from you for a little while was that you might have him back forever (aiōnion).' The use of aiōnion (eternal, forever) shifts from the temporal separation (a little while, pros hōran) to the eschatological dimension: in Christ, the relationship between Philemon and Onesimus is not merely restored but transformed into a permanent brotherly bond that death itself will not end. The providential interpretation of Onesimus's separation — 'perhaps this was God's purpose' — echoes Joseph's interpretation of his Egyptian slavery in Gen 45:5-8: what humans meant as harm, God used for good.
1.17: 'So if you consider me a partner (koinōnon), welcome him as you would welcome me.' The conditional 'if you consider me a partner' is rhetorically irenic: Paul is not demanding but testing whether Philemon truly shares the partnership he has professed. Koinōnos is commercial language (a business partner who shares both profit and liability) as well as friendship language. The request to welcome (proslabou) Onesimus as Paul himself would be welcomed invokes the ancient Mediterranean convention of receiving a friend's representative as the friend himself: the envoy carries the person of the sender.
1.18: 'If he has done you any wrong or owes you anything, charge it to me (touto emoi ellogei).' The language is accounting (ellogei, from logos, account) — charge it to my account. This is the most explicit financial/legal term in the letter. Roman law gave masters the legal right to recover damages from anyone who employed or harbored a runaway slave; Paul is preemptively accepting the financial liability that Philemon would legally be entitled to claim. The nature of Onesimus's wrong is not specified — theft is often inferred from v. 18, but it could simply be the economic loss of his labor during the period of absence.
1.19: 'I, Paul, am writing this with my own hand. I will pay it back — not to mention that you owe me your very self.' The autograph note is a standard Greco-Roman device for giving a letter legal weight: the handwriting of the principal was the guarantee. Paul's letters were normally dictated to a secretary (amanuensis) and signed at the close (1 Cor 16:21; Gal 6:11; Col 4:18; 2 Thess 3:17). The IOU — 'I will pay it back' — converts the letter into a legally binding instrument. The counter-claim — 'you owe me your very self' — reminds Philemon that Paul is his spiritual father (he was converted through Paul's ministry), creating an asymmetric debt that dwarfs any financial obligation Onesimus incurred.
1.20: 'I do wish, brother, that I may have some benefit (onaimēn) from you in the Lord; refresh my heart (splanchna) in Christ.' The verb onaimēn is a deliberate play on the name Onesimus — 'let me have onesimos (profit/benefit) from you.' Paul is using Onesimus's name as a verb to indicate that the ultimate benefit Paul seeks is not financial but relational: the refreshment of his own splanchna (the emotional thread running from v. 7 through v. 12 to here). The request completes the emotional arc of the letter.
1.21: 'Confident of your obedience, I write to you, knowing that you will do even more than I ask.' The phrase 'even more than I ask' has been read as an implicit request for manumission (freeing Onesimus) even though Paul has not stated it explicitly. Ancient readers and commentators (Chrysostom, Jerome) consistently read this as the implied but unstated request behind the entire letter. Whether or not manumission was the specific request, Paul's confidence in Philemon's generosity creates social obligation: to fall short of 'even more' would be to disappoint the stated expectation of a trusted friend and apostle.
1.22: 'And one thing more: Prepare a guest room (xenian) for me, because I hope to be restored (charizomai) to you in answer to your prayers.' Paul's announcement of a planned visit functions as an accountability mechanism: he will come and see how Philemon responded to the letter. The term xenian (guest room) was the standard request in Philemon-style personal letters; offering lodging was a primary form of social patronage in a world without commercial inns of acceptable quality for elite travelers. The verb charizomai (to be given as a grace/gift) frames Paul's release from prison as a divine gift mediated by Philemon's prayers — a subtle inversion of the patron-client relationship.
1.23: 'Epaphras, my fellow prisoner in Christ Jesus, sends you greetings.' Epaphras appears in Col 1:7 as the founder of the Colossian church ('our dear fellow servant, who is a faithful minister of Christ on our behalf') and in Col 4:12 as 'one of you' (a Colossian) who is 'always wrestling in prayer' for the Colossian community. His designation as 'fellow prisoner' (synaichmalōtos) may indicate actual imprisonment with Paul or use the military metaphor of a soldier captured in battle for Christ's service.
1.24: The four co-workers: Mark is almost certainly John Mark of Acts 12:12, 25; 13:13; 15:37-39 — the young man whose departure from the first missionary journey caused the Paul-Barnabas split, who is now reconciled and present with Paul (cf. 2 Tim 4:11: 'Get Mark and bring him with you, because he is helpful to me in my ministry'). Aristarchus appears in Acts 19:29; 20:4; 27:2 as a Macedonian (Thessalonian) companion of Paul. Demas appears again in 2 Tim 4:10 with the sad note that 'Demas, because he loved this world, has deserted me.' Luke is the beloved physician of Col 4:14 and the companion who remained with Paul when others departed (2 Tim 4:11: 'Only Luke is with me').
1.25: 'The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit.' The singular 'your spirit' (tou pneumatos sou) in a letter addressed to multiple people (Philemon, Apphia, Archippus, the house church) is unusual and deliberate: the grace-benediction is directed to Philemon specifically, personalizing the close. The entire letter has been an exercise in personal, relational appeal; the final benediction maintains that intimacy while extending the grace of Christ as the ultimate resource for the response Paul has requested. The last word is Christ's grace — the foundation and the goal of the entire negotiation.
